title: Greg Sherwood MW: Can SA’s fine wine progress continue in tougher times?
summary:
South Africa’s wine finds itself in a curious position at the moment as its exports to the UK, its single largest market, keep earning more money while shipping less liquid. So, after a long chat over the phone this week to a famous Stellenbosch producer, I thought it would be interesting to drill down on […]

The post Greg Sherwood MW: Can SA’s fine wine progress continue in tougher times? appeared first on winemag.
content:
South Africa’s wine finds itself in a curious position at the moment as its exports to the UK, its single largest market, keep earning more money while shipping less liquid. So, after a long chat over the phone this week to a famous Stellenbosch producer, I thought it would be interesting to drill down on this subject a little further, ahead of my upcoming trip to the Cape, as I am sure to be asked to offer further commentary on this topic.

Recent industry figures show that in 2025 the UK still accounted for roughly a fifth of South Africa’s total export value even as shipped volumes fell by around 6% to 7% year on year. Industry body Wines of South Africa (WOSA) has openly framed this shift as a deliberate strategy, to prioritise value over volume and retreat somewhat from the bargain-bin battlefield of bulk wine, preferring to fight for space on the fine wine shelves and listings in on-trade wine lists.

The question this raises is bigger than the trade statistics themselves. As European wine consumption contracts and the ageing cohort of serious collectors reportedly drift back towards Bordeaux, Burgundy, Piedmont and the Rhône, can South Africa’s premium category really establish itself as a peer to the old world’s classics, or is it destined to remain a well-regarded but secondary player in the fine wine market?

The backdrop: falling consumption, rising premiumisation

Certainly, looking around the UK market, the broader context is unforgiving. UK wine consumption supposedly peaked back in 2009, and has been sliding ever since, with overall alcohol volumes continuing to soften even as total market value edges up on premiumisation.

Younger drinkers are not replacing the older ones at anything like the same rate, with mindful drinking habits continuing to spread and ready-to-drink cocktails and No/Lo alcohol alternatives eating into wine’s share of the drinks trolley. Even wider afield in France, wine’s spiritual fine wine home, its market it not immune, with recent data showing still wine volumes falling there too, with the red wine category hardest hit.

In other words, South African is not competing in a growing market against entrenched incumbents, it is competing for a larger slice of a market that is itself getting smaller for almost everyone. Paradoxically, that shared pain is possibly part of what makes South Africa’s positioning more coherent.

If the market for cheap, everyday wine is contracting fastest as we are told, and if the surviving demand is concentrating in the premium and super premium tiers, then a producing country’s fortunes depend less on how many bottles it can push and more on whether its top wines are credible at the dinner table alongside a Claret, or a Chianti Classico. South Africa’s export strategy of smaller volumes at better price points is essentially a bet that its reputation for quality can carry it into that shrinking but higher-value space.

The case for a positive outlook

There is of course real substance behind those betting South Africa can and will compete. Regions like Stellenbosch, Swartland, Hemel-en-Aarde and Elgin have spent almost two decades building a track record with the critics and sommeliers, and South African Chenin Blanc, Cape Bordeaux Blends and cool-climate Syrah from various regions increasingly turn up on serious wine lists rather than as house pours.

According to recent export stats, bulk wine pricing has also actually been improving even as volumes fall, which suggests underlying quality is being rewarded rather than the country simply exporting less of the same commodity juice. South Africa also benefits from a currency that keeps its top wine competitively priced relative to Burgundy or Napa Valley equivalents of similar quality. This “quality per pound” argument definitely resonates with UK consumers who are increasingly becoming more price conscious than a decade ago, even at the premium fine wine end.

There is also a strong demographic argument emerging in South Africa’s favour, not just against it. The classic ageing UK collector base that I’ve worked amongst for several decades and that I often refer to in my articles, which is reported gravitating back to blue-chip Bordeaux and Burgundy, is by definition, a shrinking and eventually non-renewing customer group.

Meanwhile, the wine trade press has been fairly consistent in describing younger UK drinkers as wanting “stories in the glass” but also provenance, authenticity and a sense of discovery, rather than simply defaulting to whatever their parents drank. That is fertile ground for a New World country like South Africa, with a genuinely interesting story, post-apartheid transformation, old vine heritage, bio-diversity conscious regenerative viticulture in the Cape’s fynbos kingdom, and a first-generation of independent smallholder producers.

None of these factors guarantee sales of course, but it is a ‘narrative asset’ that the classic regions cannot easily replicate, precisely because their story is much more grounded in continuity rather than change.

The case to consider against South African wine

But as any producer will tell you, marketing, a well-established narrative and a value-for-money proposition only go so far against the centuries of institutional prestige hotwired into regions like Bordeaux and Burgundy. Fine wine, as an asset class as much as a beverage, trades heavily on scarcity, provenance chains, and also secondary market liquidity, the very things Bordeaux first growths and Grand Cru Burgundy have spent generations entrenching through classifications, negociant systems and an active auction and investment market.

Fine wine trading indices such as Liv-ex, tracking Bordeaux first growths have remained comparatively resilient even in a soft market, while wealthy buyers keep chasing rare, sought-after bottlings and Chinese and wider Asian demand shows tentative signs of returning for established names.

South African wines, however excellent, largely sit outside this investment ecosystem, with a few exceptions. A wine buyer nearing retirement age, reallocating a fine wine portfolio back towards “safe” classical names, is not really choosing between Stellenbosch Cabernet and a Pauillac Bordeaux Cru Classé based on taste alone, they are often buying reputational security, resale confidence and status signalling that many South African wines still simply do not have sufficiently.

There is also a structural fragility at home in South Africa, with a shrinking vineyard area, smaller harvests, and a domestic market under real economic pressure, helping to limit South Africa’s ability to consistently supply enough top-tier wine to seed that long-term prestige-building process – something, industry leaders like Mike Ratcliffe, have been opining about for years now.

Old world regions can lean on centuries of stock, library vintages and a self-reinforcing culture of collecting, while South Africa is still, relatively speaking, building up that new, post-apartheid era fine wine archive one vintage at a time.

A challenging global market but signs for optimism

As is normally the case in economic discussions like this, the honest answer is perhaps a little more nuanced than many commentators in Europe tend to let on – that South Africa’s premium wine category can compete but perhaps in a more specific and limited sense. It can absolutely win on the merits at the dinner table, in blind tastings (as I know all too well), and increasingly among sommeliers and adventurous drinkers who value quality and a story over simple ‘brand security.’

What it cannot yet do, at least not within a single generational cycle, is displace the old-world classics as the default choice of the risk-averse collector guarding their capital in a difficult economy.

As the market shrinks, South Africa’s realistic path to growth is not head-to-head combat with Bordeaux and Burgundy for the same conservative buyers, but capturing the more curious, value-conscious, and younger segment of a fine wine market that the classics are still, for the most part, too expensive by default.

South Africa’s premium producers simply need to become more proficient at operating in, and marketing to, these fine margins of the consumer fine wine category. Therein lies future long-term success.

  • Greg Sherwood was born in Pretoria, South Africa, and as the son of a career diplomat, spent his first 21 years traveling the globe with his parents. With a Business Management and Marketing degree from Webster University, St. Louis, Missouri, USA, Sherwood began his working career as a commodity trader. In 2000, he decided to make more of a long-held interest in wine taking a position at Handford Wines in South Kensington, London, working his way up to the position of Senior Wine Buyer over 22 years. Sherwood currently consults to a number of top fine wine merchants in London while always keeping one eye firmly on the South African wine industry. He qualified as the 303rd Master of Wine in 2007.

The post Greg Sherwood MW: Can SA’s fine wine progress continue in tougher times? appeared first on winemag.

title: Tim James: The latest Sadie releases
summary:
The Swartland has, like the ANC flag, three colours: In summer there’s the pervasive ochreous gold of the harvested wheatfields, in winter the brilliant green of growing cereals and lush cover crops around bare vines; and always the hills have patches of the renosterbos whose sombre darkness the early settlers, we surmise, named this part […]

The post Tim James: The latest Sadie releases appeared first on winemag.
content:
The Swartland has, like the ANC flag, three colours: In summer there’s the pervasive ochreous gold of the harvested wheatfields, in winter the brilliant green of growing cereals and lush cover crops around bare vines; and always the hills have patches of the renosterbos whose sombre darkness the early settlers, we surmise, named this part of the country for – when there was no wheat or vine to push it aside, and the newcomers had not yet shot all the zebra-like creatures for which they named the Paardeberg.

It was the Paardeberg I was driving towards, to taste the latest Sadie releases at the home-farm. And as a symbol of the expected and much-feared drought, there on the left was a huge green field of wheat being irrigated by one of those vast overhead systems of pipes and wheels and water. Irrigation, in the middle of the Cape winter! Drought is a looming threat to the grape-farmers too in this dryland area – though I’d been earlier in the week at the Mullineux’s Roundstone farm, where the dams are full, thanks to the run-off from the Kasteelberg mountains during the April downpour.

The Sadie dam is well short of full and, introducing the tasting, Eben spoke of what he called the current “groen droogte”, or green drought. But we were there to sample, mostly, wines from last year’s altogether benign harvest. For once, it seems, we can safely generalise about a vintage across the whole of the Cape: 2025 was great, and Eben spoke of how promisingly wonderful the grapes looked as they arrived at the cellar.

We started with the reds, as usual, and Eben warned that the first two were reduced (a complex matter read this piece by Jamie Goode if you want to know more). It can produce some off-odours, sometimes paradoxically welcome ones, and Eben says that time in bottle will deal with the “problem” – even six months should help. All the more reason to not drink these or any of the wines early, though aeration via splashy decanting or vigorous swirling should serve.

With Soldaat, the Piekenierskloof grenache, I thought the aromas were rather masked at first, but the characteristic red fruit (cherries, cranberries) came through with a lovely perfume. With more substance than the wine occasionally has, it seemed an excellent example: nervy and understated but not too light and certainly not austere; quite airy yet the flavours intense and lingering.

The aromas of Swartland cinsault Pofadder I found more difficult to penetrate, but there’s no doubt from the palate about how good the wine is and I’m sure the aromas will regain their glory. It’s richly textured, with a sweet charm at its core that’s also a succulent dry and complex fruitiness intertwined with tannins of great finesse. A joy to have in the mouth.

Eben Sadie in the young Sonvang vineyard.

The second vintage of Sonvang, the multi-varietal blend off home-farm youngish vines, is very pleasing too, a touch more “obvious” than the two wines just tasted, and with the feeling that something is being held back (waiting for more vine maturity perhaps?). Full-fruited with spicy and herbal overlay, a hint of youthful rawness and a firm tannic presence. Should benefit from a good few years in bottle.

Introducing Treinspoor, as always Eben argued his belief in the potential of tinta barocca to make great varietal wines, although this is inevitably the hardest sell in the range, he says. And, as always, I edge closer to being convinced, without quite arriving there. It’s intense, serious wine with big but sufficiently balanced tannins and persistent dark-fruit flavours. Balance yes, but seamless harmony no. This will come and the wine will undoubtedly age well and beneficially; tasting it in ten or 15 years will make one regret not having bought a case of the stuff.

The final red was Columella, from the rather more difficult year 2024. Difficulties, if any, have been surmounted, and the wine is presenting beautifully. Floral, syrah-suggestive perfume, not overstated, with dried herbal notes. Very complete and remarkably drinkable now, I fear, although I thought the tannic structure (steely but Swartland-characteristically melting around the fruit) more forward than it often is, though there’s plenty of flesh and presence.

And so to the excitement of the whites. Skerpioen as always showed the saltiness that the chalky soils on the edge of the sea up the Atlantic Coast  produces, and a characteristic bright lemony acidity. But although it has finesse and focus, with a dry stoniness, there’s a lot of fruit-forward flavour – with chenin at this stage showing more than its equal partner palomino, I thought.

‘T Voetpad is always wonderful for me, and that’s only partly because I know the extraordinary, unique vineyard alone in its far corner of the Swartland. It’s rich and full, with ripe flavours, yet also restrained, the generous texture matched by a grippy structure. Altogether intense and profound, and well worth laying down.

Eben didn’t mention reduction on Kokerboom, but I thought there was some, light and even rather attractive “gunflint”. But there’s no denying the zesty lemon and lanolin of semillon (mostly white, some red), along with a sweet-fruited core that’s almost peachy in the midst of a stony minerality. As always, perhaps even more so here, a special wine from the magical (if rather scruffy) Skurfberg Citrusdal Mountains.

The Skurfberg connection was broken (so the chenins could be tasted together), by a return to the Rotsvas farm on the Paardeberg and the other field blend, Twiswind. Again, the argument with the wind was won by the vines – all dozen varieties of them, gathered in just two pickings, for the earlier and the later ripening.  Pretty aromas I thought, with both apple fruit and apple blossom present. Grippy and firm, with some character, complexity and charm. Surely this is going to be a great vineyard.

The chenins then. Skurfberg shows a lot of sweet-fruited ripeness, with even a tropical edge of pineapple alongside the peach, at 14.5% alcohol – though Eben says that that is not an unusual level for this wine. The firm, grippy acidity holds it all together, however, and it’s fresh as well as textured, and the structure carries the fruit to a long finish. This should be as popular a wine as ever.

If the home farm has some great emerging fieldblends, it already has a truly splendid chenin vineyard. The 2025 is the fourth bottling of Rotsbank and probably the finest, which is claiming a lot. A stone-and-steel expression of chenin, pure and brilliant, superbly balanced, both elegant and gorgeous, really alive. Only Mev Kirsten could not be a disappointment after that wine, but this very different expression of chenin was as satisfactory as ever. An incredible intensity of sweet fruit in its lovely, refined elegance, though “intense” seems almost the wrong word for something as gracefully weightless as this. It reminded me, though I’d be hard put to explain the connection, of a German dry auslese riesling.

And to complete a remarkable concluding trio (which was for me the highlight of this truly impressive tasting) was Palladius 2024. Eben’s notes list 15 varieties for it, from 17 vineyards; made in clay pots, concrete tank and old wooded foudre. Could it be less than complex, to say the least? To mention lemon and peach (as my inadequate notes do) is just to make a start. Penetratingly intense, without losing refinement; lifted and light-feeling.

  • Tim James is one of South Africa’s leading wine commentators, contributing to various local and international wine publications. His book Wines of the New South Africa – Tradition and Revolution appeared in 2013.

The post Tim James: The latest Sadie releases appeared first on winemag.

title: Editorial: Not every good wine has to be rare
summary:
South African wine has become remarkably successful at producing world-class bottles at the top end and inexpensive supermarket wines at the bottom. But what has happened to the space between commodity wine and ultra-premium offerings, where many consumers begin to trade up? One fairly recent wine that attempts to address this gap is Boschendal Nicolas, […]

The post Editorial: Not every good wine has to be rare appeared first on winemag.
content:
South African wine has become remarkably successful at producing world-class bottles at the top end and inexpensive supermarket wines at the bottom. But what has happened to the space between commodity wine and ultra-premium offerings, where many consumers begin to trade up?

One fairly recent wine that attempts to address this gap is Boschendal Nicolas, a red blend launched by producer-wholesaler DGB. The maiden 2016 vintage was released in 2019 with a high-profile, luxury-focused campaign, the apparent aim being to create a brand offering superior quality and status to mass-market wines, but at a price point significantly below traditional luxury offerings.

Then selling for R200 a bottle, Nicolas challenged conventional terroir thinking by being classified as W.O. Coastal Region, and appeared aimed at emerging-market urban professionals while retaining sufficient quality and character to satisfy enthusiasts.

On quality, I wrote at the time: “Quite winning. Red and black fruit, a subtle herbal note, some floral perfume on the nose. The palate is medium bodied with fresh acidity and very fine tannins, the finish admirably dry. Not super-intense but balanced and supple.” I awarded it 90 points.

Critics questioned whether a relatively modestly priced wine could credibly occupy a luxury position, despite the brand ambition. Another issue was scale. Initial production was just 20,000 litres, or 26,666 bottles.

Commenting on this site, Mike Ratcliffe of Vilafonté and a seasoned industry player argued that South Africa needed globally recognised brands capable of selling millions of bottles annually. “The market is so big that Chocolate Block [from Boekenhoutskloof] and R&R Classique will never even notice the competition. The South Africa dream should be that we develop at least 10 single branded wines selling 2 million-plus bottles at the R200/$20/£12-15 price point or more – this would be a massive success. It can be done – quite quickly. This would start putting us on the map,” he wrote.

Fast forward to 2026 and the 2023 vintage of Nicolas (38% Cabernet Sauvignon, 29% Shiraz, 26% Merlot, 5% Cabernet Franc, 1% Malbec and 1% Petit Verdot and now designated W.O. Stellenbosch) has just been judged best overall in the Signature Red Blend Report sponsored by Prescient Fund Services, scoring 95 points. For those inclined to dismiss blind tastings as a bit of a lottery, it is worth noting that the same wine was awarded Best Other Red Blend at this year’s Trophy Wine Show, scoring 96 points.

Volumes are not yet at the 2-million-bottle mark, but they have grown to 210,000 bottles, just shy of 700% growth over eight vintages. Price, meanwhile, has risen to R240 a bottle, an increase of 20%.

A few observations:

  1. Volume growth is genuinely impressive.
    Moving from 26,666 bottles to 210,000 bottles in eight vintages is exceptional. Many premium wine brands struggle to move beyond niche status. Nicolas has achieved meaningful consumer acceptance helped no doubt by canny distribution and brand activation.
  2. The icon ambition remains a work in progress.
    If the goal was to create a globally recognised South African super-brand selling one to two million bottles annually, there is still some way to go. At 210,000 bottles, Nicolas is a successful premium brand; at two million bottles, it becomes an industry case study.
  3. The pricing strategy is intriguing.
    A move from R200 to R240 over eight vintages is modest, particularly after inflation. In real terms, the wine has probably become cheaper. This could be read as DGB prioritising accessibility and volume over maximising price. Equally, the relatively modest increase may say more about difficult trading conditions than any conscious pricing strategy.
  4. The numbers are not to be sneezed at.
    The project moved from approximately R5.3 million in gross sales (26,666 bottles × R200) to R50.4 million (210,000 bottles × R240): just less than a tenfold increase. That is a significant commercial achievement.

My verdict? Nicolas has cleared the first hurdle. The interesting bit now begins.

Nicolas has become more than yet another red blend. It has developed into a genuine brand. The next challenge is whether it can cross the threshold from successful premium proposition to international-scale icon.

The danger would be celebrating too early. At 210,000 bottles, the brand has clearly gained traction. At two million bottles, it becomes transformative.

The kicker came this past weekend while visiting my parents-in-law in Montagu. Having woefully undercatered on wine to accompany our lamb shank lunch, I was quite happy to pick up a bottle of Nicolas from the local TOPS at SPAR. It is poised and unshowy, and at 13.5% alcohol, very much in tune with the idea that less can be more.

The post Editorial: Not every good wine has to be rare appeared first on winemag.

title: Hartenberg appoints Nongcebo Langa to the winemaking team
summary:
Prominent Stellenbosch property Hartenberg has announced on Facebook that Nongcebo Langa has joined its winemaking team. Langa was previously winemaker at Delheim, understudy to Roelof Lotriet. She joins Hartenberg following the departure of long-serving head winemaker Carl Schultz, who left at the beginning of last year after more than three decades at the property, having […]

The post Hartenberg appoints Nongcebo Langa to the winemaking team appeared first on winemag.
content:
Prominent Stellenbosch property Hartenberg has announced on Facebook that Nongcebo Langa has joined its winemaking team. Langa was previously winemaker at Delheim, understudy to Roelof Lotriet. She joins Hartenberg following the departure of long-serving head winemaker Carl Schultz, who left at the beginning of last year after more than three decades at the property, having served since 1993. He was succeeded by Reynie Oosthuizen, formerly winemaker at Kleinood, where he oversaw the Tamboerskloof range.

The post Hartenberg appoints Nongcebo Langa to the winemaking team appeared first on winemag.

title: Vondeling appoints Callie Louw as consulting winemaker
summary:
Vondeling has appointed Callie Louw, previously of Swartland property Porseleinberg, as viticultural advisor and consulting winemaker, following the departure of Emile van der Merwe, who spent 14 years at the Paarl property. Louw is no stranger to Vondeling, having served as the Voor Paardeberg property’s first winemaker between 2005 and 2007, when he made its […]

The post Vondeling appoints Callie Louw as consulting winemaker appeared first on winemag.
content:
Vondeling has appointed Callie Louw, previously of Swartland property Porseleinberg, as viticultural advisor and consulting winemaker, following the departure of Emile van der Merwe, who spent 14 years at the Paarl property.

Louw is no stranger to Vondeling, having served as the Voor Paardeberg property’s first winemaker between 2005 and 2007, when he made its earliest vintages, including the inaugural release of Babiana white blend.

In his new role, Louw will advise on the long-term winemaking direction of both Vondeling and sister property Vrymansfontein, where the first wines from recently established vineyards are due for release.

Louw recently left Porseleinberg after acquiring a property on the Paardeberg, where he is establishing his own project, Sonkop.

The post Vondeling appoints Callie Louw as consulting winemaker appeared first on winemag.
